**CHAPTER ONE**   
**INTRODUCTION**

**1.1 Historical Background**  
	The **Eulogio “Amang” Rodriguez Institute of Science and Technology (EARIST)** was established in **1945**, following the liberation of Manila. Originally founded as a vocational-technical school, EARIST has grown into a state institution recognized for its excellence in science, technology, and innovation. Over the years, it has expanded its academic programs to include engineering, education, business, architecture, and the arts. Since its inception, the institution has been committed to fostering both academic and non-academic growth among its students, guided by its mission to produce competent, value-oriented, and globally competitive graduates.

This Study focuses on the **Student Affairs and Services (SAS): Leadership Division**, which plays a critical role in promoting the holistic development of students beyond the classroom. SAS focuses on managing the non-academic aspects of student life by providing programs and services that support student welfare, leadership, and overall development. Recognized by the **Commission on Higher Education (CHED)** as a vital component of higher education institutions, SAS adheres to the standards set forth in **CHED Memorandum Order No. 09, Series of 2013**, which mandates essential services such as guidance and counseling, scholarships, career placement, health programs, and co-curricular activities.

The **Leadership Division** of SAS plays a key role in empowering student organizations and governments. This division ensures that College **Based Organization (CBOs), Institute Based Organization (IBOs),** and **College Student Governments (CSGs)** are accredited accordingly and function effectively by managing  their activities, resources, and compliance with institutional policies.

The  **Student Affairs and Services (SAS)** of **Eulogio “Amang” Rodriguez Institute of Science and Technology (EARIST)** works closely with various colleges and organizations to enhance student engagement and leadership development.

List of the Clients:

**Institute Student Government**

* Institute Student Government \- Executive Department  
* Institute Student Government \- Legislative Department

**College Student Governments:**

* College of Computing Studies  
* College of Architecture and Fine Arts  
* College of Arts and Sciences  
* College of Business and Public Administration  
* College of Education  
* College of Engineering  
* College of Hospitality and Tourism Management  
* College of Industrial Technology  
* College of Criminal Justice Education

**Accredited Organizations:**

* Fine Arts Student Society (FASS)  
* Student Association of Interior Design (SAID)  
* United Architects of the Philippines – Student Auxiliary EARIST Chapter (UAPSA-EC)  
* Applied Physics Society (APSOC)  
* Elite Students of Psychology Association (ESPA)  
* Samahang Sipnayan (SS)  
* Human Resource Society (HRS)  
* Junior Marketing Society (JMS)  
* Office Administration Society (OAS)  
* Public Administration Society (PAS)  
* Young Entrepreneurs Society (YES)  
* Criminologia Discipuli Societas (CDS)  
* Computer Science Student Association (COMSA)  
* Information Technology Students' Association (ITSA)  
* Kapisanan ng Diwa at Panitik (KaDiPan)  
* Math Thinkers' Club (MTC)  
* Science Club (SC)  
* Special Educators' Club (SEC)  
* Student Teaching Organization (STO)  
* Technology and Livelihood Education Student Society (TLE SS)  
* Association of Computer Engineering Students (ACES)  
* Civil Engineering Students’ Association (CESA)  
* EARIST \- Chemical Engineering Students' Society (EARIST-ChESS)  
* Electronics Engineering Students Society (ECESS)  
* Institute of Integrated Electrical Engineers \- Society of Electrical Engineering Students (IIEE-SEES)  
* Philippine Society of Mechanical Engineers – EARIST Student Unit (PSME-ESU)  
* Society of EARIST Tourism Management Students (SETS)  
* Society of EARIST Hospitality Management Students (SEHMS)  
* EARIST Junior Fashion and Apparel Technologist Student Society (EJFATSS)  
* Crimson Vulpes Esports (CVE)  
* EARIST Anihan Dance Troupe (EADT)  
* EARIST Chamber Orchestra (ECO)  
* EARIST Chorale (EC)  
* EARIST Rondalla (ER)  
* EARIST Theatre Arts Guild (ETAG)  
* EARIST Vocal Artist Guild (EVAG)  
* Red Fox Dance Company (RFDC)  
* EARIST College "Y" Club (ECYC)  
* EARIST \- Samahan ng mga Iskolar ng Kagawaran ng Agham at Teknolohiya (EARIST-SIKAT)  
* Sphere of Archimedes (SOA)

**1.3 Statement of the Problem**

**Problem 1: Delayed Approval Duration and Insufficient Clarity in Activity Requirements**

**Documents Concerned**

* **Accomplishment Report** \- Periodic summaries of completed projects and activities submitted by accredited organizations and College Student Governments (CSGs).  
* **Financial Report** \- Details the income and expenses of organizations for specific projects or activities..  
* **Financial Statement** \- Provides a comprehensive summary of the financial position of organizations, including assets, liabilities, and fund utilization.

**Scenario**:  Student organizations submit activity proposals for approval; however, the process is protracted and lacks transparency. Proposals are manually routed across multiple offices, and student leaders are unable to monitor the status of their requests in real time. Consequently, this often results in approvals being granted at the last minute, compromising event planning

**Cause**

* Absence of a unified system for submitting and tracking proposals.  
* Student leaders receive no real-time updates regarding their proposal’s progress.  
* Sequential (one-by-one) approval procedures significantly slow down the workflow.

**Impact**

* Delayed approvals lead to insufficient preparation time for events.  
* Student leaders expend considerable effort conducting multiple in-person follow-ups.  
* It becomes difficult to determine which office is causing delays within the approval chain.

## **Problem 2: Inefficient Documentation and Tracking of Incoming and Outgoing Documents**

### **Documents Concerned:**

* **Incoming Documents:** Project proposals, compliance reports, financial documents, activity plans, and official communications from AOs, ISGs, and CSGs.  
* **Outgoing Documents:** Approvals, notices, endorsements, feedback, and directives issued by the SAS Office.

### **Scenario**

The EARIST Student Affairs and Services (SAS) Office is facing challenges in managing the large and continuous flow of incoming and outgoing documents submitted by Accredited Organizations (AOs), Institute Student Governments (ISGs), and College Student Governments (CSGs). The current documentation system relies heavily on manual processes, making it difficult to verify whether documents were submitted or released on time.

Due to the absence of a structured and centralized tracking mechanism, issues such as misplaced documents, delayed responses, and lack of accountability frequently occur. These challenges hinder the SAS Office’s capacity to maintain an organized workflow and provide timely outputs to student organizations and governing bodies.

### **Cause:**

* Absence of a centralized digital system for tracking submissions and outgoing documents.  
* Dependence on manual record-keeping methods, which are susceptible to human error.  
* Insufficient search functionalities, resulting in protracted document retrieval times..

### **Impact:**

* **Delayed processing:** Processing delays arising from challenges in locating and verifying documents, affecting approvals, endorsements, and feedback.  
* **Accountability issues:** Accountability deficiencies, as untracked or misplaced documents generate confusion among AOs, the ISG, and CSGs.  
* **Operational inefficiencies:**  Operational inefficiencies, with SAS staff dedicating excessive time to manual searches and record verification.

**Problem 3: Inefficient Management and Monitoring of Equipment Borrowing**

**Documents Concerned:**

* **Equipment Request Form** – The official form submitted by accredited organizations and college student governments for borrowing SAS-owned materials.  
* **Borrowing Slip/Gate Pass** – The document issued to authorize the release and subsequent return of borrowed items.  
* **Equipment Inventory List and Return/Inspection Checklist** – The master record of all SAS-owned equipment and the standardized form used to verify the condition of items upon return.

**Scenario:** Accredited Organizations **(AOs)** and College Student Governments **(CSGs)** regularly borrow equipment under the custody of the Student Affairs and Services **(SAS)** Office, principally **chairs**, **LED walls**, **flags**, **electric fans**, as well as **sound systems**, **microphones**, **extension cords**, **tables**, and other ancillary materials required for institutional activities. The entire borrowing process is conducted through manual, paper-based forms and physical logbooks. The absence of a digitized platform precludes real-time visibility of item availability, current borrower details, reservation conflicts, and return compliance, thereby giving rise to recurrent operational inefficiencies.

**Causes**

* Lack of a centralized, automated equipment inventory and borrowing management system.  
* Continued dependence on manual ledger entries and paper-based tracking mechanisms prone to error and omission.  
* Absence of automated notification protocols for impending return deadlines and systematic flagging of overdue or damaged items.

**Impacts**

* Persistent double-booking and unavailability of high-demand assets, particularly LED walls, chairs, and electric fans during peak academic periods.  
* Undetected loss or deterioration of institutional property owing to inadequate accountability and post-use inspection procedures.  
* Significant administrative burden placed on SAS personnel in conducting physical inventory verification, resolving conflicts, and pursuing overdue returns.

**1.4 Scope and Delimitation**

**Scope**

This study puts emphasis on improving the existing system developed by a previous batch of junior college students for the Student Affairs and Services (SAS) Office: Leadership Division of the Eulogio “Amang” Rodriguez Institute of Science and Technology, which streamlines and digitizes operational processes dealing with organizational reports, document tracking, and equipment monitoring.

The scope of this project includes:

* **System Enhancement:** The enhancement of the user interface and overall usability to ensure easier navigation and accessibility for SAS staff, RSOs, and CSGs.  
* **Feature Optimization:** Enhance the existing features—report submission monitoring, document tracking, and equipment borrowing management—by increasing precision, efficiency, and automation.  
* **Notification System Upgrade:** This enhancement of the notification and alert system will allow for better and more timely reminders on upcoming deadlines and overdue items.  
* **Data Management and Reporting:** Improved data recording, retrieval, and reporting to aid in decision-making and reduce manual workloads.  
* **Security Enhancements:** Stronger authentication and authorization for users is necessary to protect data integrity and confidentiality.

**Delimitation**

The improved system will be confined within the SAS Office at the Manila campus of EARIST and shall not cover other departments or satellite campuses. The access to the system shall be allowed only to SAS staff, ISG, and CSG, AOs representatives; general students and external users are excluded. The system enhancement will focus only on enhancing the existing modules and will not include the integration of third-party services or mobile applications in this current stage. The equipment monitoring feature includes only the equipment and materials owned by the SAS Office, such as LED walls, chairs, and related assets. 

Moreover, the system requires an active internet connection for proper operation since offline or local deployment functionalities are out of scope for the current study. This enhanced system seeks to enhance system reliability and efficiency, building on a better user experience while remaining within the core structure and purpose of the system for which it was developed.

**CHAPTER TWO**  
**SYSTEM ANALYSIS**

**2.1 System Name and Background**  
	The Eulogio ‘Amang’ Rodriguez Institute of Science and Technology (EARIST) Student Affairs and Services Enhanced Leadership Division System is a centralized institutional system designed to support the administrative and developmental functions of the Student Affairs and Services Office, specifically its Leadership Division. The division manages all non-academic student leadership activities, including the accreditation of student organizations, monitoring of College Student Governments and the Institute Student Government, and processing of essential organizational documents. Currently, the division handles these processes manually, relying heavily on paper-based submissions, physical document routing, and manual record-keeping.

The Enhanced Leadership Division System integrates the core workflows of the Student Affairs and Services Leadership Division. These workflows include the submission and approval of student activity proposals, verification of financial and accomplishment reports, tracking of incoming and outgoing documents, monitoring of borrowed equipment, and the generation of summary reports needed for compliance and evaluation. The purpose of the system is to streamline and digitize the entire operational process in order to ensure efficient handling of student organization requirements and to improve transparency in administrative procedures.

Internal entities of the system include personnel from the higher administrative offices down to the organization representatives. This includes the Student Affairs Director, the Student Affairs and Services Coordinators, the Leadership Division Head, staff members assigned to documentation and equipment management, and the officers of the Institute Student Government, College Student Governments, and Accredited Organizations. These users interact with the system according to their respective roles in submitting, reviewing, approving, and monitoring organizational documents and activities.

**2.2 System Analysis Tools**

	**2.2.1 System Outline**

  I. Request and Initiation of Services

1. Submission of service requests from student organizations and administrative units.  
2. Initial review of the request by the assigned division (Training, Documentation, Evaluation, Engagement).  
3. Clarification of incomplete or unclear requirements with the requesting party.  
4. Logging of the request and initial verification by the Division Head.  
5. Approved requests proceed to scheduling and planning; unapproved requests are returned with justification.

II. Needs Assessment and Planning

1. Conduct assessment based on the type of service requested.  
     
2. Compilation of required materials and logistics:  
- Venue, equipment, manpower  
- Digital forms (registration, attendance, evaluation)  
- Training modules or certificates  
3. Assignment of responsible officers for planning and execution.  
4. Preparation of timeline and activity workflow for endorsement.

III. Approval and Confirmation

1. Submission of final activity plan to the Student Affairs and Services (SAS) Director for approval.  
2. Dissemination of confirmation to the requesting party, including schedule and requirements.  
3. Revision of the plan if returned with comments for adjustment.

IV. Preparation and Resource Allocation

1. Preparation of operational materials:  
- Attendance sheets  
- Evaluation forms  
- Certificates  
- Training documents  
2. Coordination with other units such as:  
- Facility management  
- ICT for technical support  
- Security and protocol (if necessary)  
3. Daily monitoring and logging of materials and logistics readiness.

V. Implementation of Services

1. Facilitation of leadership training, seminars, documentation, and other requested services.  
2. Adjustment of materials or processes if shortages or issues arise.  
3. Real-time documentation through attendance tracking, photos, and reports  
4. Conducting participant evaluations and feedback sessions.

VI. Post-Activity Documentation and Reporting

1. Compilation of essential documents:  
- Attendance report  
- Evaluation results  
- Narrative report  
- Photo documentation  
2. Review and validation by the Division Head.  
3. Submission of completed documentation to SAS for official recording.  
4. Archiving of files by semester, year, and activity type.

VII. Issuance of Certificates, Records, and Endorsements

1. Preparation of certificates and endorsements based on verified participation.  
2. Securing necessary signatures from the Division Head or SAS Director.  
3. Release of documents to the student; digital copies stored in the archive.  
4. Verification and correction for re-issuance if errors are reported.

VIII. Daily Operations Reporting

1. Updating of daily log entries for completed activities and services.  
2. Tracking usage of supplies and resources.  
3. Recording leadership cases handled and documents issued.  
4. Monitoring activity progress and linking to ongoing programs.

IX. Monthly Inventory and Performance Monitoring

1. Monthly inventory check of supplies, equipment, and documents.  
2. Comparison of current and previous month’s usage for performance evaluation.  
3. Updating digital records for transparency and audit purposes.  
4. Submission of monthly accomplishment reports to SAS.

**2.2.2  System Flowchart**

1. Existing System

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

   

**Figure 2.1: Existing System Flowchart**  
	This flowchart illustrates the process flow of the **existing** EARIST SAS Leadership Division web-based system. It begins with user login, followed by access to the Admin Dashboard, where users manage document monitoring, equipment tracking, and notifications. The system automates deadline tracking, records document transactions, and ensures proper monitoring of borrowed equipment to improve efficiency.

2. Proposed System

**Figure 2.2: Proposed System Flowchart**  
This flowchart illustrates the process flow of the proposed EARIST SAS Leadership Division web-based system. The system enhances the existing process by introducing a more streamlined and integrated wo\`rkflow, starting from secure user authentication to role-based access within the dashboard. Authorized users can efficiently manage document submissions, monitor report deadlines, track borrowed equipment, and receive real-time notifications. The proposed system emphasizes improved automation, data accuracy, and centralized monitoring to support faster decision-making and more efficient administrative operations within the Leadership Division.  
**B. Admin Workflow**

**Figure 2.3: Admin Workflow**  
The admin workflow describes the sequence of actions performed by system administrators within the proposed web-based system. After successful login, the admin accesses the dashboard, which serves as the central control panel for system operations. From this interface, the admin manages document monitoring, updates report statuses, tracks deadlines, and records incoming and outgoing transactions. The admin is also responsible for monitoring borrowed equipment, verifying returns, and generating notifications related to overdue documents and materials. This workflow ensures proper oversight, data accuracy, and efficient coordination of administrative tasks within the Leadership Division.

**2.2.3 Gantt Chart**

                              **Figure 2.4: Gantt Chart.**   
This Gantt chart illustrates the project timeline by detailing the major phases, including planning, system design, development, testing, and deployment. It functions as a project management tool that monitors progress and supports the timely accomplishment of all project tasks.

**2.2.4. Context Diagram**  
**![][image1]**  
**Figure 2.6: Context Diagram – EARIST SAS Leadership Division System**  
	The **Context Diagram** presents a high-level representation of the **EARIST SAS Leadership Division System** and its interaction with external entities. It illustrates how **Accredited Organizations (AOs)**, **College Student Governments (CSGs)**, and the **Institute Student Government (ISG)** submit reports, activity proposals, document requests, and equipment borrowing requests to the system.   
The enhanced system processes these submissions, tracks incoming and outgoing documents, manages equipment inventory, and provides real-time feedback to users. It enables seamless communication between student organizations and the SAS Leadership Division by delivering automated notifications, transparent status monitoring, and structured data management. Overall, the diagram establishes a foundational view of the system’s data flows and user interactions, serving as a guide for understanding the scope and functionality of the enhanced platform.

**2.2.5. Data Flow Diagram**

1. Existing System

![][image2]  
**Figure 2.7: Dataflow Diagram – Existing System**  
The Data Flow Diagram of the existing system shows how document records, report submissions, and equipment borrowing information are manually processed and stored by the EARIST SAS Leadership Division. Data is recorded in separate files and used for monitoring documents and equipment, while follow-ups and notifications are handled manually. This diagram highlights the basic flow of information and the limited level of automation in the current system.

**B. Proposed System**

**Figure 2.8: Dataflow Diagram – Proposed System**

	The improved Dataflow Diagram (DFD) illustrates a comprehensive enhancement of the SAS Leadership Division’s information management system. In contrast to the original DFD, which depicted a fragmented workflow dependent on manual entry and physical logs, the proposed DFD integrates new modules such as a formal proposal approval process, centralized document and equipment monitoring, automated notifications, and secure user role management. These redesigned flows ensure better accuracy, faster processing, and increased transparency across all transactions. By automating critical tasks and unifying data handling, the improved DFD significantly strengthens operational efficiency and stakeholder communication.

# **2.2.6 Data Dictionary**

**Table 2.1: Filling Incoming and Outgoing Communication**

| FIELD NAME | FIELD  TYPE | FIELD LENGTH | DECIMAL | FORMAT | DESCRIPTION |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Number** | Numeric | 5 | 0 | X(5) | Number of the Paper |
| **Date** | Datetime | 9 | 0 | mm/dd/yy | Date Received |
| **Time** | Datetime | 5 | 0 | 99:99 | Time Received |
| **Office** | Alphabet | 40 | 0 | X(20) | The name of the Org, CSG, or Office |
| **Letter** | Alphanumeric | 300 | 0 | X(300) | Brief Summary of the Letter |
| **Received By** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Name of the Receiver |
| **Filled By** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Name who filled the file |
| **Outgoing By** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Person name who outgoing the paper |

This table outlines the data structure used to monitor incoming and outgoing documents. It contains fields such as document number, date, time, organization name, document summary, and assigned personnel, ensuring accurate documentation and efficient retrieval.

**Table 2.2: System Notification for Due Date of Requirements.**

| FIELD NAME | FIELD TYPE | FIELD LENGTH | DECIMAL | FORMAT | DESCRIPTION |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Date** | Datetime | 9 | 0 | mm/dd/yy | Date Received |
| **Time** | Datetime | 5 | 0 | 99:99 | Time Received |
| **Letter** | Alphanumeric | 300 | 0 | X(300) | Text in the Notification |

This table presents the data fields used to generate system notifications for submission deadlines. It supports timely alerts for accomplishment reports, financial reports, and compliance documents, helping prevent delays in submissions.

**Table 2.3: Equipment Monitoring Module.** 

| FIELD NAME | FIELD  TYPE | FIELD LENGTH | DECIMAL | FORMAT | DESCRIPTION |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Number** | Numeric | 5 | 0 | X(5) | Number of the Paper |
| **Name** | Alphabet | 50 | 0 | X(50) | Borrower Name |
| **Date** | Datetime | 9 | 0 | mm/dd/yy | Date Received |
| **Time** | Datetime | 5 | 0 | 99:99 | Time Received |
| **Date** | Datetime | 9 | 0 | mm/dd/yy | Date of event execution  |
| **Date** | Datetime | 9 | 0 | mm/dd/yy | Date returned of the borrowed items |
| **Name** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Inspection Officer |
| **Item 1** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Number of borrowed old chairs |
| **Item 2** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Number of borrowed new chairs  |
| **Item 3** | Alphabet | 20 | 0 | X(20) | LED Wall |
| **Findings** | Alphanumeric | 20 | 0 | X(20) | Number of broken items  |
| **Comments** | Alphanumeric | 300 | 0 | X(300) | Comments on the borrowing happened |

This table defines the data fields used to track borrowed equipment. It includes borrower details, item descriptions, borrowing and return dates, inspection information, and remarks to ensure accountability and prevent equipment loss or damage.

**2.2.7 User Requirements**  
**Personnel:**

- SAS Staff: Responsible for overseeing system operations, monitoring deadlines, and ensuring compliance.   
- RSO and CSG: Submit reports, borrow equipment, and access notifications. 


**Existing Computer Specifications:**   
Number of personal computers (for company use): 6

| Software Specifications  | **Edition:** Windows 11 Pro  **Web Browser:** Chrome and Microsoft Edge  **Network type:** Access point  WPA2 Personal  |
| Personnel Requirements  | Any personnel proficient at personal computer  |

**CHAPTER THREE**  
**GENERAL DESIGN**  
**3.1 General Objectives**   
The objective of this research is to create, develop, and implement a centralized approval and documentation system that improves the SAS Leadership Division at EARIST's activity-related submissions, such as Accomplishment Reports, Financial Reports, and Financial Statements, in terms of timeliness, clarity, and dependability. The system seeks to increase cooperation between SAS personnel, Recognized Student Organizations, and College Student Governments, expedite approval procedures, eliminate processing delays, and lower errors brought on by ambiguous requirements.

By offering structured logs, real-time progress tracking, and easily available documentation for auditing and evaluation reasons, the study also aims to improve transparency, bolster record management, and assist evidence-based decision-making. It will be expected that this strategy will result in a procedure for managing all activity-related submissions that is more effective, accountable, and user-friendly.

**3.2 Specific Objectives**   
To achieve the general objective, the study focuses on the following specific objectives:

1. To enhance the approval process by creating an organized workflow that specifies procedures, necessary paperwork, and accountable individuals for each evaluation stage.  
2. To reduce delays and enhance follow-ups, automate submission monitoring by incorporating real-time notifications, status updates, and deadline reminders.  
3. To implement checklist and validation tools that assist organizations in confirming the accuracy and completeness of their papers before submission.  
4. To improve accountability and transparency by implementing a system that records revision requests, decision dates, and reviewer activities.  
5. To guide RSOs and CSGs in creating accurate and comprehensive documentation by offering user orientation and support materials.  
6. to assess how well the solution reduces processing time, enhances document quality, and boosts user happiness among SAS employees and student organizations.

**3.3 Presentation of Alternatives**   
The following alternatives were considered to address the identified challenges effectively:  
**3.3.1 Alternative No. 1 \- Stand Alone Set-up**

This alternative is a Stand-Alone Document Management System developed using VB.NET with a local SQL database. It operates on a single workstation and does not require internet or network connectivity.

The system is intended for authorized SAS staff and is installed on designated office computers. Users must log in using valid credentials to access the system. After authentication, users are directed to a dashboard where they can manage documents, reports, and borrowed equipment through dedicated modules.

All transactions are validated and stored in a local database to ensure data accuracy and integrity. The system provides local alerts for pending reports and overdue equipment, records user activities for accountability, and ends each session with a secure logout process. The application functions fully as long as the user is logged in, making it suitable for offices with limited or no internet access.

	To achieve the general objective, this study focuses on the following specific objectives:

1. Report Submission Monitoring: To automate the recording and monitoring of submission schedules for organizational reports, enabling SAS staff to track pending and overdue requirements efficiently.  
2. Document Record Management: To establish a centralized local database for managing incoming and outgoing documents, allowing systematic encoding, retrieval, and generation of document records.  
3. Equipment Borrowing Tracking: To monitor borrowed equipment by recording borrower information, borrowing dates, and expected return schedules, and to identify overdue items.  
4. Stand-Alone System Support: To support daily SAS office operations through a stand-alone VB.NET application installed on designated computers without reliance on internet connectivity.  
5. Security and Data Integrity: To ensure data security, accuracy, and accountability by implementing user authentication, validation mechanisms, and activity logging within the system.

**3.3.1.1 System Flowchart (Stand Alone Set – up)**

**Figure 3.1: Flowchart \- Standalone Set-Up**

**Figure 3.1: Flowchart (Standalone set-up)**

This flowchart presents the process flow of the improved stand-alone Document Management System developed using VB.NET. The system begins with user authentication, where authorized SAS staff enter their login credentials. Once validated, the user is redirected to the main dashboard, which functions as the central interface for accessing system modules such as Document Tracking, Report Management, Equipment Monitoring, and Utilities. Each module allows users to encode, update, and manage records related to organizational reports, incoming and outgoing documents, and borrowed equipment.

All transactions performed within the system are validated and stored in a local SQL database to ensure data integrity and consistency. detect overdue equipment returns, and prevent invalid or duplicate entries. The workflow ends with a secure logout, ensuring proper session termination and controlled system access.

**3.3.1.2 Physical Elements**   
**3.3.1.2.1 Network Layout (Stand Alone Set – up)** 

**Figure 3.2: Network Layout (Stand Alone Set – up)**  
This network layout illustrates the Student Affairs Service (SAS) office in a school, showing how desktop PCs, laptops, IP phones, printers, and a server are interconnected via routers, switches, and a Wi-Fi network. The configuration is designed to support efficient communication, reliable data management, and secure internet access across the office.  
**3.3.1.7 Graphical User Interface (Standalone Set-up)** 

**Figure 3.3: Graphical User Interface (VB.NET) \- Log in form**  
**![][image3]**  
**Figure 3.4: Graphical User Interface (VB.NET) \- Main and Dashboard**

Figure 3.3 and figure 3.4 showcases  the user interface of a Document Management System developed in Visual Basic (VB) for the management of liquidation reports, document tracking, and borrowed equipment records. The interface is organized with a Navigation Menu that provides access to core system modules, including transactions, reports, maintenance, utilities, history, and system information. Positioned below the menu, the Toolbar offers quick-access functions such as opening downloaded files and generating reports.

**3.3.2 Alternative No. 2 \- Centralized Set-up**

The centralized set-up functions as the core system supporting all SAS operations by connecting multiple offices and enabling real-time access to critical information. A central server hosts the system database, ensuring data consistency, integrity, and security across all transactions. System access is controlled through predefined user roles, allowing only authorized personnel to perform sensitive operations.

The system automates key processes such as report deadline monitoring, document flow tracking, and equipment management. It also generates notifications and detailed reports to assist management in decision-making. By centralizing data and operations, the system minimizes manual errors, improves operational efficiency, and enhances coordination between SAS staff and student organizations.

To achieve the general objective, this study focuses on the following specific objectives:

1. Centralized Report Monitoring:  To provide real-time tracking of organizational report submissions through a centralized database, enabling SAS staff to monitor pending and overdue reports efficiently.  
2. Centralized Document Management: To maintain a shared system for recording and managing incoming and outgoing documents, allowing consistent record-keeping, easy retrieval, and reporting.  
3. Centralized Equipment Tracking**:** To monitor borrowed equipment and maintain schedules for returns using a centralized database, ensuring timely follow-up and reducing losses.

4. Multi-User System Accessibility: To allow authorized SAS personnel and relevant student organizations to access the system simultaneously based on assigned roles, promoting collaboration and operational efficiency.  
5. Data Security and Integrity: To ensure the accuracy, consistency, and security of information through centralized authentication, role-based access, validation, and audit logging.  
6. Operational Efficiency and Decision Support: To streamline SAS operations, reduce manual errors, and provide actionable reports and notifications that support management decision-making.

**3.3.2.1 System Flowchart (Centralized Set – up)** 

**Figure 3.5: Flowchart \- Centralized Setup**  
The centralized setup flowchart illustrates how all users interact with a single central system that manages authentication, transaction processing, data storage, and notifications. Unlike a standalone setup, where processing may occur independently across offices, the centralized system ensures real-time synchronization, consistent data handling, and automated workflows. This setup improves efficiency, security, and accessibility across all SAS Leadership Division operations.

**3.3.2.2 Physical Elements**  
**3.3.2.2.1 Network Layout (cedrick)**

**Figure 3.6: Network Layout (Centralized Setup)**  
This network layout represents a hybrid network infrastructure that integrates both cloud-based services and local server connections. A central server interfaces with the cloud and distributes resources to multiple workstations. Each workstation is connected to printers and other peripheral devices, enabling efficient data sharing and communication. The overall structure follows a hierarchical topology, optimizing data flow, resource allocation, and centralized device management across the system.

**3.3.2.7 Graphical User Interface (Centralized Set-up)** 

**Figure 3.7: Welcome Page (Centralized Set-up)**

The Welcome Page serves as the initial interface for users when they access the system. It provides a brief introduction to the system, which is designed for managing student affairs and services. The page contains a Login button that directs users to the login page, ensuring that only authorized personnel can access the system’s functionalities. The layout is simple and user-friendly, providing a clear entry point for users.

**Figure 3.8: Login Page (Centralized Set-up)**

The Login Page is the authentication interface of the system. Users are required to enter their username and password to access the system. This ensures that only registered and authorized users can log in, maintaining the security and integrity of the system. A “Back to Home” option is also provided for users who may wish to return to the Welcome Page without logging in. This page is critical for restricting access and protecting sensitive information within the system.

**![][image4]**  
**Figure 3.9: Dashboard (Centralized Set-up)**

The Dashboard serves as the main interface for users after logging into the system. It provides a centralized view of all essential functions, allowing users to efficiently manage incoming and outgoing documents, monitor activity proposals, track borrowed equipment, access SAS forms, and consult the student handbook. Each module is clearly displayed for easy navigation, enabling users to perform tasks quickly while maintaining organization and accountability within the Student Affairs and Services operations. The dashboard is designed to streamline administrative processes and ensure that critical information is readily accessible in one interface.

**3.3.3 Alternative No.3 (Web-Based Set-Up)**

Web-based design enables system functions to be accessed through a browser-based platform, allowing users to interact with the system across different devices and locations. Authentication, transaction processing, data storage, and notification services are handled through the web interface, ensuring consistent system behavior and a uniform user experience.

Real-time data processing and seamless information sharing are supported in a web-based environment, which improves data accuracy and timeliness. Automated workflows reduce manual tasks, limit errors, and enhance operational efficiency. Security measures such as role-based access control and server-side validation contribute to the protection of sensitive information and system reliability.

Within the context of the SAS Leadership Division, a web-based approach supports accessibility, scalability, and ease of maintenance. Multiple users can operate within the same system environment while maintaining reliable data management and coordinated workflows suitable for institutional operations.

**3.3.3.1 System Flowchart**  
**![][image5]**  
**Figure 3.10: System Flowchart**  
The system flowchart illustrates the overall sequence of operations in the proposed EARIST SAS Leadership Division web-based system. It begins with user login, followed by OTP verification to ensure secure authentication. Once verified, users gain access to the dashboard where authorized actions such as document monitoring, equipment tracking, and notification management are performed. The flowchart shows how the system processes user requests, updates records, and completes tasks in a secure and efficient manner.

**3.3.3.2 System Flowchart**  
**Figure 3.11: System Flowchart**

This system flowchart illustrates the comprehensive workflow accessible through the SAS Admin Dashboard, specifically focusing on three primary operational modules: Incoming Document Management, Outgoing Document Management, and Activity Proposal Processing. This flowchart illustrates how administrative personnel from the Student Affairs and Services Office interact with the system to manage the flow of organizational documents and oversee the submission and processing of student activity proposals. The workflow originates from a centralized dashboard interface, denoted by point "B," which serves as the main navigation hub for SAS administrative functions.

**3.3.3.3 System Flowchart**

**Figure 3.12: System Flowchart**

depicts the administrative workflow for managing three essential operational components of the Student Affairs and Services Office: SAS Forms, Student Handbooks, and Borrowed Equipment tracking. This flowchart, also originating from the SAS Admin Dashboard (point "B"), illustrates the system's capability to handle diverse administrative responsibilities through specialized modules that each address specific operational needs. The integration of these functions into a single dashboard interface exemplifies the system's commitment to operational efficiency and centralized management.

 

**3.3.3.4 System Flowchart**

**Figure 3.13: System Flowchart**

illustrates the workflow accessible through the Student Officer Dashboard (point "C"), which serves student organization leaders including CSG officers, ISG representatives, and accredited organization (AO) leaders. This flowchart demonstrates the system's dual-access architecture, wherein student leaders can independently access certain functions without requiring direct administrative intervention, promoting autonomy while maintaining appropriate oversight and accountability mechanisms. The dashboard provides student officers with three primary functional areas: Activity Proposal submission, access to SAS Forms and Student Handbooks, and Equipment Borrowing request management.

 

	**3.3.3.2 Physical Elements**  
**3.3.3.2.1 Network Layout**  
**![][image6]**  
**Figure 3.14 Network Layout**  
The network layout illustrates the web-based setup of the proposed EARIST SAS Leadership Division system. Users access the system through web browsers over a secure network connection, while the application server handles authentication, processing, and system logic. All data is stored in a centralized database server, allowing real-time access and updates. This setup supports secure communication, centralized data management, and reliable system availability for authorized users.  
**Table 3.8: Users Table**

| Field Name | Data Type | Description | Constraints |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| user\_id | INT | Unique user ID | Primary Key |
| username | VARCHAR(50) | Login name | Unique, Not Null |
| password\_hash | VARCHAR(255) | Encrypted password | Not Null |
| role | ENUM | Org, Staff, Director | Not Null |
| organization | VARCHAR(100) | Org name (if applicable) | Nullable |
| status | ENUM | Active, Inactive | Not Null |

**Table 3.9: Reports Table**

| Field Name | Data Type | Description | Constraints |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| report\_id | INT | Unique report ID | Primary Key |
| organization | VARCHAR(100) | Submitting org | Not Null |
| report\_type | ENUM | Accomplishment, Financial | Not Null |
| submission\_date | DATE | Date submitted | Not Null |
| deadline | DATE | Report deadline | Not Null |
| status | ENUM | Submitted, Late, Reviewed | Not Null |
| reviewed\_by | INT | SAS Staff | Foreign Key (Users) |

**Table 3.10: Proposals Table**

| Field Name | Data Type | Description | Constraints |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| proposal\_id | INT | Unique proposal ID | Primary Key |
| organization | VARCHAR(100) | Submitting org | Not Null |
| title | VARCHAR(150) | Activity title | Not Null |
| submission\_date | DATE | Date submitted | Not Null |
| status | ENUM | Pending, Approved, Revision | Not Null |
| reviewed\_by | INT | SAS Staff | Foreign Key |
| approved\_by | INT | Director | Foreign Key |
| remarks | TEXT | Review comments | Nullable |

**Table 3.11: Documents TableTable 3.12: Equipment\_Transactions Table**

| Field Name | Data Type | Description | Constraints |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| transaction\_id | INT | Unique transaction | Primary Key |
| equipment\_name | VARCHAR(100) | Item name | Not Null |
| organization | VARCHAR(100) | Borrower | Not Null |
| action | ENUM | Borrow, Return | Not Null |
| transaction\_date | DATE | Date of action | Not Null |
| due\_date | DATE | Return deadline | Nullable |
| condition\_notes | TEXT | Item condition | Nullable |

| Field Name | Data Type | Description | Constraints |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| document\_id | INT | Unique document ID | Primary Key |
| organization | VARCHAR(100) | Source org | Not Null |
| document\_type | VARCHAR(100) | Document description | Not Null |
| direction | ENUM | Incoming, Outgoing | Not Null |
| date\_logged | DATE | Date recorded | Not Null |
| handled\_by | INT | SAS Staff | Foreign Key |
| remarks | TEXT | Notes | Nullable |

The centralized database consolidates all system data into five core tables that support authentication, report management, proposal approval, document tracking, and equipment transactions. By minimizing redundancy and integrating related information, the database enables real-time access, consistent data handling, and simplified maintenance. This design supports the operational needs of the SAS Leadership Division while remaining efficient and easy to manage.

**3.3.2.3 Requirement Definition (Web-Based Set-up)** 

|                         ITEMS |     REQUIRED  |        EXISTING |      NEEDED  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  **Hardware Specification ● Processor:** 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13400 2.50 GHz **● Installed Ram:** 8.00 GB **● System Type:** 64-bit operating system, x64-based processor **● Storage:** 1 TB SSD**●** Secondary Storage:  512 SSD **Software Specifications ● Edition:** Windows 11 Pro **● Web Browser:** Chrome and Microsoft Edge **●** Network type: Access point **●** WPA2 Personal **Back-up ●** Toshiba 500GB Portable Hard 6 6 6 6 6 0 0 0 6 53 Drive (USB 3.0 & 2.0) Canvio Go **Printer** |                        **1            1            1            6** |                        **0             0              0             6** |            **6           6             6            0** |

This section describes the hardware and software requirements for the centralized configuration of the TraffiPro system. It compares the required, existing, and additional components necessary to ensure the system operates efficiently and reliably.

**3.3.2.6 Tangible and Intangible Benefits (Web-Based)**

1. Faster access to data across departments and authorized users  
2. Reduced data duplication and improved data consistency  
3. More efficient monitoring of equipment and document transactions  
4. Easier system maintenance and data management through a single database  
5. Improved coordination and collaboration among departments  
6. Enhanced transparency and accountability in organizational processes  
7. Increased efficiency in decision-making due to unified data access  
8. Improved service quality and professionalism  
9. Higher user satisfaction from streamlined and consistent procedures

**3.3.3.6 Graphic User Interface**  
**![][image7]**  
**Figure 3.12 Login Page (GUI)**  
The GUI login page serves as the system’s entry point, providing a user-friendly interface for authentication. Users enter their credentials and complete OTP verification to securely access the system. The design emphasizes clarity and ease of use, guiding users efficiently to the dashboard while ensuring secure login procedures  
**![][image8]**  
**Figure 3.13 Registration Page (GUI)**  
The GUI registration page allows new users to create an account in the system through a user-friendly interface. Users provide required information, which is validated and stored securely in the database. The design ensures ease of use, accurate data entry, and secure onboarding of authorized users into the web-based system.  
**![][image9]**  
**Figure 3.14 Admin Dashboard (GUI)**  
The Admin Dashboard provides a central interface for administrators to monitor and manage system activities. It displays an overview of documents, equipment, notifications, and user actions, enabling efficient control and quick access to all key operations.  
**![][image10]**  
**Figure 3.15: User Dashboard (GUI)**  
The User Dashboard provides verified users with a personalized interface to access all system features. It displays relevant documents, equipment status, notifications, and request updates, allowing users to efficiently track their activities and interact with the system.
